 {"id":"bd-1ak","title":"EPIC: ORM Relationship System","description":"# ORM Relationship System

## Overview
Implement full ORM relationship support comparable to SQLAlchemy/SQLModel. This is THE defining feature that separates an ORM from a mere query builder with type mapping.

## Why This Matters
Without relationships, users must:
- Manually write JOINs for every related query
- Manually track foreign keys
- Lose the core ergonomic benefit of an ORM (`hero.team` should just work)

Python SQLModel provides:
```python
class Hero(SQLModel, table=True):
    team_id: Optional[int] = Field(foreign_key="team.id")
    team: Optional["Team"] = Relationship(back_populates="heroes")

class Team(SQLModel, table=True):
    heroes: List["Hero"] = Relationship(back_populates="team")

# Usage: hero.team automatically fetches the related Team
```

## Rust Design Philosophy
Instead of runtime reflection magic, we use:
1. **Compile-time type safety** via proc macros
2. **Explicit relationship definitions** that generate correct code
3. **Optional lazy loading** (user opts in, not default)
4. **Eager loading support** for performance-conscious queries

## Target API (Rust)
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table = "heroes")]
struct Hero {
    #[sqlmodel(primary_key, auto_increment)]
    id: Option<i64>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(foreign_key = "teams.id")]
    team_id: Option<i64>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship(model = "Team", foreign_key = "team_id"))]
    team: Related<Team>,  // or Lazy<Team> for lazy loading
}

#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table = "teams")]
struct Team {
    #[sqlmodel(primary_key, auto_increment)]
    id: Option<i64>,
    
    name: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship(model = "Hero", back_populates = "team"))]
    heroes: RelatedMany<Hero>,
}
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] One-to-many relationships work (Team has many Heroes)
- [ ] Many-to-one relationships work (Hero belongs to Team)
- [ ] Many-to-many with link tables work
- [ ] Eager loading with JOINs works
- [ ] Optional lazy loading works
- [ ] back_populates synchronization works
- [ ] Cascade operations work via FK constraints
- [ ] Type-safe at compile time

## Architecture Decisions
1. **Related<T> vs Lazy<T>**: Two wrapper types - Related for eager-loaded, Lazy for deferred
2. **No automatic lazy loading by default**: Explicit is better for Rust, prevents N+1 surprises
3. **Macro-generated accessors**: The relationship macro generates the fetch methods
4. **Connection context required**: Must pass connection/session to load related data

## Dependencies
- Core Model trait (done)
- Query builder with JOINs (done)
- Session/Unit-of-Work (separate epic, can work without but better with)

## Estimated Scope
- 8-12 subtasks
- Touches: sqlmodel-core, sqlmodel-macros, sqlmodel-query
- New types: Related<T>, RelatedMany<T>, Lazy<T>, RelationshipInfo","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-27T20:12:54.238244618Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T00:04:07.379475967Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T00:04:07.379414012Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-1cr","title":"Field Attributes: Complete All 40+ Parameters","description":"## Overview

Implement ALL Field() parameters from Python SQLModel/Pydantic.

## Python Field() Parameters (Complete List)

### Basic Parameters
- default: Any - Default value for field
- default_factory: Optional[Callable] - Factory function for defaults
- alias: Optional[str] - Input/output alias
- validation_alias: Optional[str] - Input-only alias (Pydantic v2)
- serialization_alias: Optional[str] - Output-only alias (Pydantic v2)
- title: Optional[str] - Field title in schema
- description: Optional[str] - Field description in schema
- exclude: Union[set, Mapping] - Exclude from model operations
- include: Union[set, Mapping] - Include in model operations
- schema_extra: Optional[dict] - Extra schema configuration
- const: Optional[bool] - Constant value constraint
- repr: bool - Include in repr (default: True)

### Validation Parameters
- gt, ge, lt, le: Optional[float] - Numeric bounds
- multiple_of: Optional[float] - Must be multiple of value
- max_digits: Optional[int] - Max decimal digits
- decimal_places: Optional[int] - Decimal precision
- min_items, max_items: Optional[int] - Collection size bounds
- unique_items: Optional[bool] - Items must be unique
- min_length, max_length: Optional[int] - String length bounds
- allow_mutation: bool - Allow field mutation
- regex: Optional[str] - Regex pattern validation
- discriminator: Optional[str] - Union type discrimination

### SQLAlchemy Column Parameters
- primary_key, nullable, unique, index - Column constraints
- sa_type - SQLAlchemy type override
- sa_column - Full SQLAlchemy Column (mutually exclusive with other SA params)
- sa_column_args, sa_column_kwargs - Extra Column() args/kwargs
- foreign_key, ondelete, onupdate - Foreign key config

## Current Status

Many are implemented but gaps exist in:
- Alias system (validation_alias, serialization_alias)
- Schema metadata (title, description, schema_extra)
- Collection validators (min_items, max_items, unique_items)
- Full sa_column override support
- multiple_of validation
- discriminator support

## Implementation Notes

Each parameter must:
1. Be parseable from #[sqlmodel(...)] or #[field(...)] attributes
2. Generate appropriate SQL DDL where applicable
3. Generate validation code where applicable
4. Work with serde for serialization
5. Have tests covering all edge cases","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:59:11.095624519Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T16:58:19.317457938Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1cr","depends_on_id":"bd-162","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:58:19.317440856Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1cuq","title":"Implement horizontal sharding","description":"## Description

Partition data across multiple databases.

## Python Behavior

```python
from sqlalchemy.ext.horizontal_shard import ShardedSession

class ShardedDB:
    def shard_chooser(self, mapper, instance, clause=None):
        if instance:
            return f'shard_{instance.region}'
        return 'shard_default'
    
    def id_chooser(self, query, ident):
        return ['shard_us', 'shard_eu']  # Search all
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
pub struct ShardedPool {
    shards: HashMap<String, Pool>,
    chooser: Box<dyn ShardChooser>,
}

pub trait ShardChooser: Send + Sync {
    fn choose_for_insert<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> String;
    fn choose_for_query(&self, query: &Query) -> Vec<String>;
}

// Example: shard by user_id modulo
struct ModuloShardChooser {
    shard_count: usize,
}

impl ShardChooser for ModuloShardChooser {
    fn choose_for_insert<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> String {
        let id = model.shard_key();
        format!("shard_{}", id % self.shard_count)
    }
}
```

## Shard Key

Models need shard key:
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(shard_key = "user_id")]
struct Order {
    user_id: i64,
    // ...
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ShardedPool implementation
- [ ] ShardChooser trait
- [ ] Shard key on models
- [ ] Cross-shard queries
- [ ] Shard-aware transactions

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/sharding.rs)
- [ ] Test shard key extraction
- [ ] Test shard routing function
- [ ] Test cross-shard query handling
- [ ] Test shard configuration

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/horizontal_sharding.rs)
- [ ] Query routes to correct shard
- [ ] Insert routes by shard key
- [ ] Cross-shard aggregation
- [ ] Shard rebalancing
- [ ] Shard failover

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Shard routing decision
- [ ] INFO: Shard selection
- [ ] WARN: Cross-shard query detected

-{"id":"bd-1cy","title":"Implement Window Functions (OVER clause)","description":"## Description

Support window functions with OVER clause.

## Python Behavior

```python
from sqlalchemy import func, over

# Row number
stmt = select(
    User.name,
    func.row_number().over(order_by=User.created_at).label('row_num')
)

# Partition by
stmt = select(
    Order.id,
    func.sum(Order.amount).over(partition_by=Order.customer_id).label('customer_total')
)

# Window frame
stmt = select(
    Sale.date,
    func.avg(Sale.amount).over(
        partition_by=Sale.product_id,
        order_by=Sale.date,
        rows=(-2, 0)  # Current row and 2 preceding
    )
)
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
let query = select!(User)
    .column(Expr::function("row_number", vec![])
        .over()
        .order_by(User::created_at.asc())
        .as_("row_num")
    );

let query = select!(Order)
    .column(Order::amount.sum()
        .over()
        .partition_by(Order::customer_id)
        .as_("customer_total")
    );

// With frame
let query = select!(Sale)
    .column(Sale::amount.avg()
        .over()
        .partition_by(Sale::product_id)
        .order_by(Sale::date.asc())
        .rows_between(-2, 0)  // ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    );
```

## Window Functions to Support

- ROW_NUMBER()
- RANK()
- DENSE_RANK()
- NTILE(n)
- LAG(expr, offset)
- LEAD(expr, offset)
- FIRST_VALUE(expr)
- LAST_VALUE(expr)
- NTH_VALUE(expr, n)

Plus aggregates with OVER:
- SUM(), AVG(), COUNT(), MIN(), MAX()

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] over() creates window function
- [ ] partition_by() works
- [ ] order_by() works
- [ ] rows_between() for frame
- [ ] range_between() for frame
- [ ] All window functions supported
- [ ] Correct SQL for each dialect

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-query/src/window.rs)
- [ ] Test ROW_NUMBER() OVER generation
- [ ] Test RANK() and DENSE_RANK()
- [ ] Test PARTITION BY clause
- [ ] Test ORDER BY within OVER
- [ ] Test frame specification (ROWS/RANGE)
- [ ] Test LAG/LEAD functions
- [ ] Test aggregate functions in OVER

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/query_window.rs)
- [ ] SELECT with ROW_NUMBER() ranks correctly
- [ ] PARTITION BY groups correctly
- [ ] Running sum with SUM() OVER
- [ ] Complex window: RANK() PARTITION BY x ORDER BY y
- [ ] Multiple window functions in same query
- [ ] Window function in subquery

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Window clause SQL generation
- [ ] TRACE: Frame specification details

 {"id":"bd-1dv","title":"Implement field alias system (alias, validation_alias, serialization_alias)","description":"## Description

Add support for field aliases matching Pydantic behavior.

## Python Behavior

```python
class User(SQLModel):
    internal_id: int = Field(alias='id')
    user_name: str = Field(
        validation_alias='username',  # Accept 'username' on input
        serialization_alias='userName'  # Output as 'userName'
    )
```

## Rust Implementation

### Macro Attributes
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    #[sqlmodel(alias = "id")]
    internal_id: i32,
    
    #[sqlmodel(
        validation_alias = "username",
        serialization_alias = "userName"
    )]
    user_name: String,
}
```

### Generated Code

For validation_alias:
- Accept the alias during deserialization
- Map to actual field name

For serialization_alias:
- Output using alias during serialization

### Serde Integration

Use #[serde(alias = "...")] for validation_alias
Use #[serde(rename = "...")] for serialization_alias

## Files to Modify

- crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/parse.rs - Parse new attributes
- crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/model.rs - Generate serde attributes
- crates/sqlmodel/tests/alias.rs - Add tests

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] alias attribute works for both input and output
- [ ] validation_alias only affects input
- [ ] serialization_alias only affects output
- [ ] Works with JSON serialization
- [ ] Works with model_validate/model_dump
- [ ] Comprehensive tests

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/field.rs)
- [ ] Test alias parsed from attribute
- [ ] Test validation_alias only used for input
- [ ] Test serialization_alias only used for output
- [ ] Test populate_by_name allows both
- [ ] Test alias conflicts detected
- [ ] Test alias with special characters

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/field_aliases.rs)
- [ ] JSON with alias name → model_validate works
- [ ] model_dump with by_alias=true uses aliases
- [ ] validation_alias + serialization_alias asymmetric
- [ ] Database column uses serialization_alias
- [ ] API round-trip with aliases

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Alias resolution during parse
- [ ] TRACE: Field name mapping decisions

 {"id":"bd-1fs","title":"Computed Fields and Hybrid Properties","description":"## Overview

Implement computed fields and hybrid properties from Pydantic/SQLAlchemy.

## Pydantic Computed Fields

```python
from pydantic import computed_field

class User(SQLModel):
    first_name: str
    last_name: str
    
    @computed_field
    @property
    def full_name(self) -> str:
        return f'{self.first_name} {self.last_name}'
```

### Behavior
- Computed at access time (not stored in DB)
- Included in model_dump() unless exclude_computed_fields=True
- Can be marked as cached
- Can have custom serialization

## SQLAlchemy Hybrid Properties

```python
from sqlalchemy.ext.hybrid import hybrid_property

class User(SQLModel):
    _password: str
    
    @hybrid_property
    def password(self):
        return self._password
    
    @password.setter
    def password(self, value):
        self._password = hash_password(value)
    
    @password.expression
    def password(cls):
        return cls._password
```

### Behavior
- Work both at Python/Rust level AND in SQL queries
- expression() method returns SQL expression for use in WHERE clauses
- Allows computed values to be used in database queries

## Rust Implementation

```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    first_name: String,
    last_name: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(computed)]
    full_name: Computed<String>,
}

impl User {
    fn compute_full_name(&self) -> String {
        format!("{} {}", self.first_name, self.last_name)
    }
}
```

Or with hybrid properties:
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    #[sqlmodel(hybrid, sql_expr = "age * 365")]
    age_in_days: Hybrid<i32>,
}
```","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T05:00:50.645369854Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T16:58:24.853369376Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1fs","depends_on_id":"bd-162","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:58:24.853345732Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1fz","title":"Define RelationshipInfo struct and RelationshipKind enum","description":"# Task: Define RelationshipInfo and RelationshipKind

## Context
This is the foundational data structure for the entire relationship system. It mirrors Python's RelationshipInfo but adapted for Rust's type system.

## What to Implement

### 1. RelationshipKind Enum
```rust
// In sqlmodel-core/src/relationship.rs (new file)

/// The type of relationship between two models.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum RelationshipKind {
    /// One-to-one: Hero has one Profile
    OneToOne,
    /// Many-to-one: Many Heroes belong to one Team
    ManyToOne,
    /// One-to-many: One Team has many Heroes
    OneToMany,
    /// Many-to-many: Heroes have many Powers, Powers have many Heroes
    ManyToMany,
}
```

### 2. RelationshipInfo Struct
```rust
/// Metadata about a relationship between models.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct RelationshipInfo {
    /// Name of the relationship field
    pub name: &'static str,
    
    /// The related model's table name
    pub related_table: &'static str,
    
    /// Kind of relationship
    pub kind: RelationshipKind,
    
    /// Local foreign key column (for ManyToOne)
    /// e.g., "team_id" on Hero
    pub local_key: Option<&'static str>,
    
    /// Remote foreign key column (for OneToMany)
    /// e.g., "team_id" on Hero when accessed from Team
    pub remote_key: Option<&'static str>,
    
    /// Link table for ManyToMany relationships
    pub link_table: Option<LinkTableInfo>,
    
    /// The field on the related model that points back
    pub back_populates: Option<&'static str>,
    
    /// Whether to use lazy loading
    pub lazy: bool,
    
    /// Cascade delete behavior
    pub cascade_delete: bool,
}
```

### 3. LinkTableInfo for Many-to-Many
```rust
/// Information about a link/join table for many-to-many relationships.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct LinkTableInfo {
    /// The link table name (e.g., "hero_powers")
    pub table_name: &'static str,
    
    /// Column in link table pointing to local model
    /// e.g., "hero_id" in hero_powers
    pub local_column: &'static str,
    
    /// Column in link table pointing to remote model
    /// e.g., "power_id" in hero_powers
    pub remote_column: &'static str,
}
```

### 4. Add to Model Trait
```rust
// Extend the Model trait
pub trait Model: Sized {
    // ... existing methods ...
    
    /// Relationship metadata for this model
    const RELATIONSHIPS: &'static [RelationshipInfo] = &[];
}
```

## Files to Modify
- Create: `crates/sqlmodel-core/src/relationship.rs`
- Modify: `crates/sqlmodel-core/src/lib.rs` (export new types)
- Modify: `crates/sqlmodel-core/src/model.rs` (add RELATIONSHIPS to trait)

## Testing
- Unit tests for RelationshipKind methods
- Unit tests for RelationshipInfo builder pattern
- Ensure Default impl exists

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] RelationshipKind enum with all 4 variants
- [ ] RelationshipInfo struct with all fields
- [ ] LinkTableInfo struct
- [ ] Builder pattern for RelationshipInfo (const fn where possible)
- [ ] Model trait updated with RELATIONSHIPS constant
- [ ] All types exported from sqlmodel-core
- [ ] Unit tests pass
- [ ] Documentation with examples","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T20:14:40.670335947Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T20:39:38.877211592Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T20:39:38.877144336Z","close_reason":"Completed: RelationshipKind/Info + Model::RELATIONSHIPS","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1fz","depends_on_id":"bd-1ak","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T20:14:40.688123478Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-kzp1","title":"Implement table inheritance patterns","description":"## Description

Support SQLAlchemy-style table inheritance.

## Single Table Inheritance

All subclasses in one table with discriminator:
```python
class Employee(SQLModel, table=True):
    type: str
    
class Manager(Employee):
    department: str  # Same table, type='manager'
    
class Engineer(Employee):
    specialty: str  # Same table, type='engineer'
```

## Joined Table Inheritance

Each class has own table, FKs link them:
```python
class Person(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: int
    name: str

class Employee(Person, table=True):
    employee_id: int  # FK to person.id
    department: str
```

## Concrete Table Inheritance

Each class is independent table:
```python
class ConcreteEmployee(SQLModel, table=True):
    # All fields repeated, no inheritance at DB level
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
// Single table
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inheritance = "single", discriminator = "type")]
struct Employee {
    type_: String,
}

#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(inherits = "Employee", discriminator_value = "manager")]
struct Manager {
    department: String,
}

// Joined
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inheritance = "joined")]
struct Person { ... }

#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inherits = "Person")]
struct Employee { ... }
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Single table inheritance
- [ ] Joined table inheritance
- [ ] Concrete table inheritance
- [ ] Discriminator column support
- [ ] Polymorphic queries

- [ ] Test polymorphic queries

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/table_inheritance.rs)

- [ ] Joined table: base in one, derived in another with FK
- [ ] Concrete table: each class has own table
- [ ] Query base class returns all subclasses
- [ ] Discriminator column populated correctly

- [ ] TRACE: Polymorphic query generation

 {"id":"bd-no6","title":"Integrate console into sqlmodel-mysql driver","description":"## Purpose
Add rich console output to the MySQL driver for connection progress, authentication feedback, and query visualization.

## Background
MySQL connections have unique characteristics:
- Capability negotiation (server/client flags)
- Multiple authentication plugins
- Connection attributes
- SHOW commands with formatted output
- Query warnings

## Implementation Details

### File Modifications
crates/sqlmodel-mysql/src/connection.rs
crates/sqlmodel-mysql/src/lib.rs (re-exports)

### ConsoleAware Implementation
Add optional SqlModelConsole to MysqlConnection struct.

### Connection Progress Output
Connecting to MySQL...
  [OK] TCP connected
  [OK] Handshake received (MySQL 8.0.35)
  [OK] Capabilities negotiated
  [..] Authenticating (caching_sha2_password)...
  [OK] Authenticated as user
  [OK] Selected database: myapp
  [OK] Connected (charset: utf8mb4)

### Query Timing and Warnings
Query executed in 8.5ms (10 rows returned)
Warning: Using filesort (consider adding index)

### EXPLAIN Visualization
Parse and display EXPLAIN output:
- Show query plan as tree
- Highlight full table scans
- Show index usage
- Display estimated vs actual rows

### SHOW Commands Formatting
Format common SHOW outputs nicely:
SHOW TABLES:
  - users (InnoDB, 10,234 rows)
  - posts (InnoDB, 45,678 rows)
  - sessions (MEMORY, 123 rows)

SHOW PROCESSLIST:
+----+------+-----------+------+---------+------+-------+
| Id | User | Host      | db   | Command | Time | State |
+----+------+-----------+------+---------+------+-------+
...

### Replication Status
For SHOW REPLICA STATUS:
- Lag indicator
- Position info
- Error highlighting

## Plain Text Mode
Connected to MySQL 8.0.35 at localhost:3306
Query: 8.5ms, 10 rows
Warning: Using filesort

## Conditional Compilation
Use cfg feature flag same as other drivers.

## Verification Steps
1. Test connection with console enabled
2. Test different auth plugins
3. Test EXPLAIN visualization
4. Test SHOW command formatting
5. Verify warning display
6. Test plain text output
7. Test with console disabled

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-console crate
- SqlModelConsole, Theme components","acceptance_criteria":"MySQL driver implements ConsoleAware trait

-{"id":"bd-ntpn","title":"Implement @computed_field equivalent","description":"## Description

Support computed fields that derive value from other fields.

## Python Behavior

```python
from pydantic import computed_field

class User(SQLModel):
    first_name: str
    last_name: str
    
    @computed_field
    @property
    def full_name(self) -> str:
        return f'{self.first_name} {self.last_name}'
```

## Behavior

- Computed at access time
- Included in model_dump() by default
- Can be excluded with exclude_computed_fields=True
- NOT stored in database

## Rust Implementation

### Option 1: Macro attribute
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    first_name: String,
    last_name: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(computed = "compute_full_name")]
    #[serde(skip_deserializing)]
    full_name: String,
}

impl User {
    fn compute_full_name(&self) -> String {
        format!("{} {}", self.first_name, self.last_name)
    }
}
```

### Option 2: Separate trait
```rust
impl ComputedFields for User {
    type Computed = UserComputed;
    
    fn compute(&self) -> UserComputed {
        UserComputed {
            full_name: format!("{} {}", self.first_name, self.last_name),
        }
    }
}
```

## Serialization

Generated serde:
```rust
impl Serialize for User {
    fn serialize<S>(&self, serializer: S) -> Result<S::Ok, S::Error> {
        // Include computed fields in output
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Computed fields can be declared
- [ ] Value computed from method
- [ ] Included in serialization
- [ ] Excluded from database columns
- [ ] Can exclude from dump with option

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/computed.rs)
- [ ] Test computed field declaration
- [ ] Test computed method is called
- [ ] Test computed included in serialization
- [ ] Test computed excluded from DB columns
- [ ] Test exclude_computed_fields option

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/computed_fields.rs)
- [ ] first_name + last_name → full_name computed
- [ ] Computed field in JSON output
- [ ] Computed field NOT in INSERT SQL
- [ ] model_dump(exclude_computed_fields=True) works
- [ ] Computed depends on other computed (chaining)

### Logging
- [ ] TRACE: Computed field evaluation

Will use Option 1 (macro attribute) approach for simplicity.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T05:09:12.184930028Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T17:56:36.548560957Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ntpn","depends_on_id":"bd-1fs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:57:43.589089577Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-pqg","title":"Create Console User Guide documentation","description":"## Purpose
Write a comprehensive user guide for the console feature, covering setup, configuration, and common usage patterns.

## Background
Users need a guide that takes them from zero to productive quickly, then provides depth for advanced usage. The guide should be standalone and not require reading source code.

## Implementation Details

### File Location

### Document Structure

- When to use it (human observation)
- When not to use it (automated pipelines)
- Quick comparison: with vs without console

#### 2. Quick Start (5 minutes)
- Add dependency with feature flag
- Create console and attach to session
- Run first query and see output

#### 3. Output Modes
- Rich mode: full formatting and colors
- Plain mode: text only, no ANSI
- Auto mode: detects environment
- Force mode: override detection
- Environment variables reference

#### 4. Theme Configuration
- Default themes (dark, light)
- Custom theme creation
- Color reference table
- Example themed outputs

#### 5. Renderables Catalog
- ErrorPanel: displaying errors beautifully
- QueryResultTable: formatting query results
- SchemaTree: database structure visualization
- TableInfo: single table details
- MigrationStatus: migration tracking
- Progress components: bars, spinners, trackers

#### 6. Integration Patterns
- Per-session console
- Global console
- Shared console across sessions
- Mixing console and non-console code

#### 7. Agent Compatibility
- How auto-detection works
- Environment variables for agents
- Writing agent-compatible code
- Testing with agent mode

#### 8. Troubleshooting
- Colors not showing
- Output going to wrong stream
- Performance issues
- Feature flag problems

### Writing Guidelines
- Action-oriented headings
- Code examples for every concept
- Screenshots of visual output
- Before/after comparisons
- Agent considerations throughout

## Verification Steps
1. Guide covers all major features
2. Code examples compile and run
3. Screenshots match current output
4. Technical accuracy verified
5. Newcomer can follow quick start
6. Advanced users find depth needed

## Dependencies
- All console features complete
- Visual examples for screenshots
- API stable","acceptance_criteria":"User guide covers installation and setup

 {"id":"bd-qv5","title":"Implement Session struct and lifecycle management","description":"# Task: Implement Session Struct and Lifecycle Management

## Context
The Session is the central unit of work manager. It holds a database connection, tracks objects, and coordinates flushing changes to the database. This is the most critical component of the ORM layer.

## Design Philosophy
- **Explicit over implicit**: No autoflush by default (unlike Python SQLAlchemy)
- **Ownership clarity**: Session owns or borrows connection
- **Type erasure**: Identity map stores Box<dyn Any> for heterogeneous objects
- **Transaction safety**: Atomic commit/rollback semantics

## Target API
\`\`\`rust
// Create session from pool
let session = Session::new(pool.acquire().await?);

// Add new objects (will be INSERTed on flush)
session.add(&hero);

// Get by primary key (uses identity map)
let hero = session.get::<Hero>(1).await?;

// Mark for deletion
session.delete(&hero);

// Flush pending changes to DB
session.flush().await?;

// Commit the transaction
session.commit().await?;
\`\`\`

## What to Implement

### 1. Session Struct
\`\`\`rust
// In new crate: sqlmodel-session/src/lib.rs

use std::any::{Any, TypeId};
use std::collections::HashMap;

pub struct Session {
    /// The database connection
    connection: ConnectionHolder,
    
    /// Active transaction (if any)
    transaction: Option<Transaction>,
    
    /// Identity map: (TypeId, PK) -> tracked object
    identity_map: HashMap<ObjectKey, TrackedObject>,
    
    /// Objects marked as new (need INSERT)
    pending_new: Vec<ObjectKey>,
    
    /// Objects marked as deleted (need DELETE)
    pending_delete: Vec<ObjectKey>,
    
    /// Configuration
    config: SessionConfig,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Eq)]
struct ObjectKey {
    type_id: TypeId,
    pk_hash: u64,
}

struct TrackedObject {
    object: Box<dyn Any + Send + Sync>,
    original_state: Option<Vec<u8>>,
    state: ObjectState,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum ObjectState {
    New,        // Needs INSERT
    Persistent, // Loaded from DB
    Deleted,    // Needs DELETE
    Detached,   // Removed from session
}
\`\`\`

### 2. Core Methods
\`\`\`rust
impl Session {
    pub async fn new(pool: &Pool) -> Result<Self>;
    pub fn from_connection(conn: impl Connection + 'static) -> Self;
    pub fn add<T: Model>(&mut self, obj: &T);
    pub fn delete<T: Model>(&mut self, obj: &T);
    pub async fn get<T: Model>(&mut self, pk: impl Into<Value>) -> Result<Option<T>>;
    pub async fn begin(&mut self) -> Result<()>;
    pub async fn flush(&mut self) -> Result<()>;
    pub async fn commit(&mut self) -> Result<()>;
    pub async fn rollback(&mut self) -> Result<()>;
}
\`\`\`

### 3. Query Integration
\`\`\`rust
impl Session {
    pub fn query<T: Model>(&self) -> SessionQueryBuilder<T>;
}

pub struct SessionQueryBuilder<'s, T: Model> {
    session: &'s Session,
    inner: SelectBuilder<T>,
}
\`\`\`

## Files to Create/Modify
- Create: \`crates/sqlmodel-session/Cargo.toml\`
- Create: \`crates/sqlmodel-session/src/lib.rs\`
- Create: \`crates/sqlmodel-session/src/session.rs\`
- Create: \`crates/sqlmodel-session/src/tracked.rs\`
- Modify: \`Cargo.toml\` (workspace members)

---

## Comprehensive Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (in session/src/tests.rs)

1. **Session Creation Tests**
   - \`test_session_from_connection_initializes_empty\`: No tracked objects, no pending ops
   - \`test_session_new_acquires_from_pool\`: Pool.acquire() called
   - \`test_session_config_defaults\`: Verify default SessionConfig values

2. **Object Tracking Tests**
   - \`test_add_tracks_new_object\`: add(&hero) puts hero in identity_map with ObjectState::New
   - \`test_add_same_object_twice_is_noop\`: Second add() does nothing
   - \`test_add_object_with_different_pk_tracks_separately\`: Two heroes tracked independently
   - \`test_delete_marks_persistent_for_deletion\`: delete(&hero) sets ObjectState::Deleted
   - \`test_delete_new_object_removes_from_pending\`: delete() on new object just removes it

3. **Identity Map Tests**
   - \`test_get_returns_cached_object\`: get::<Hero>(1) returns same object
   - \`test_get_fetches_from_db_if_not_cached\`: get() queries DB on cache miss
   - \`test_get_returns_none_for_nonexistent\`: get::<Hero>(999) returns None
   - \`test_get_same_pk_returns_same_instance\`: Identity guarantee

4. **Transaction Tests**
   - \`test_begin_starts_transaction\`: begin() creates Transaction
   - \`test_begin_twice_is_noop\`: Second begin() does nothing
   - \`test_commit_finalizes_transaction\`: commit() calls tx.commit()
   - \`test_rollback_discards_changes\`: rollback() calls tx.rollback()
   - \`test_commit_without_begin_is_ok\`: Implicit transaction handling

5. **Flush Tests**
   - \`test_flush_executes_inserts\`: New objects get INSERT statements
   - \`test_flush_executes_deletes\`: Deleted objects get DELETE statements
   - \`test_flush_executes_updates\`: Dirty objects get UPDATE statements
   - \`test_flush_order_delete_insert_update\`: Correct ordering prevents FK violations
   - \`test_flush_clears_pending_lists\`: pending_new/pending_delete cleared after flush

6. **State Transition Tests**
   - \`test_new_to_persistent_after_commit\`: ObjectState::New -> Persistent
   - \`test_deleted_removed_from_map_after_commit\`: Deleted objects gone
   - \`test_rollback_reverts_to_original_state\`: Original snapshot restored

### Integration Tests (tests/session_integration.rs)

1. **Full Lifecycle Tests**
   - \`test_add_flush_commit_persists_to_db\`: Full workflow with real DB
   - \`test_get_loads_from_db_into_identity_map\`: Query populates cache
   - \`test_modify_flush_updates_db\`: Change tracked object, flush, verify DB
   - \`test_delete_flush_removes_from_db\`: Delete, flush, verify gone

2. **Concurrency Tests**
   - \`test_two_sessions_same_object\`: Isolation between sessions
   - \`test_session_refresh_from_db\`: Reload object from DB

3. **Error Handling Tests**
   - \`test_commit_after_connection_error\`: Graceful failure
   - \`test_flush_with_constraint_violation\`: FK error handling
   - \`test_rollback_after_partial_flush\`: Atomicity guarantee

### E2E Test Script (tests/e2e/session_e2e.rs)

\`\`\`rust
/// E2E: Full Session workflow
/// 
/// Tests complete unit-of-work pattern:
/// 1. Create session from pool
/// 2. Add multiple objects
/// 3. Query with identity map
/// 4. Modify objects
/// 5. Delete some objects
/// 6. Flush all changes
/// 7. Commit transaction
/// 8. Verify database state
#[tokio::test]
async fn e2e_session_full_workflow() {
    // Detailed logging at each step
    tracing::info!("Starting E2E session workflow test");
    
    let pool = setup_test_pool().await;
    let mut session = Session::new(&pool).await.unwrap();
    
    // Step 1: Add new objects
    let team = Team { id: None, name: "Justice League".into() };
    session.add(&team);
    tracing::debug!(pending_new = ?session.pending_new_count(), "After add");
    
    // Step 2: Flush to get auto-generated IDs
    session.flush().await.unwrap();
    
    // Step 3: Verify identity map
    let cached = session.get::<Team>(1).await.unwrap();
    assert!(cached.is_some());
    
    // ... continue with full workflow
}

/// E2E: Multi-model relationships through Session
#[tokio::test]
async fn e2e_session_with_relationships() {
    // Create Team, Hero referencing Team
    // Verify FK constraints respected
    // Verify delete ordering (children first)
}

/// E2E: Session rollback scenario
#[tokio::test]
async fn e2e_session_rollback() {
    // Add objects, modify, then rollback
    // Verify DB unchanged
    // Verify session state reverted
}
\`\`\`

### Logging Requirements

\`\`\`rust
impl Session {
    #[tracing::instrument(level = "debug", skip(self, obj))]
    pub fn add<T: Model>(&mut self, obj: &T) {
        let key = ObjectKey::from(obj);
        tracing::info!(
            model = std::any::type_name::<T>(),
            pk = ?key.pk_hash,
            "Adding object to session"
        );
        // ...
    }
    
    #[tracing::instrument(level = "debug", skip(self))]
    pub async fn flush(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        tracing::info!(
            inserts = self.pending_new.len(),
            deletes = self.pending_delete.len(),
            "Starting flush"
        );
        
        let start = std::time::Instant::now();
        // ... flush logic ...
        
        tracing::info!(
            elapsed_ms = start.elapsed().as_millis(),
            "Flush completed"
        );
        Ok(())
    }
    
    #[tracing::instrument(level = "debug", skip(self))]
    pub async fn commit(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        tracing::info!("Committing transaction");
        // ...
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Debug Diagnostics

Add diagnostic methods for debugging:

\`\`\`rust
impl Session {
    /// Get count of objects pending INSERT
    pub fn pending_new_count(&self) -> usize { self.pending_new.len() }
    
    /// Get count of objects pending DELETE
    pub fn pending_delete_count(&self) -> usize { self.pending_delete.len() }
    
    /// Get total tracked object count
    pub fn tracked_count(&self) -> usize { self.identity_map.len() }
    
    /// Dump session state for debugging
    pub fn debug_state(&self) -> SessionDebugInfo {
        SessionDebugInfo {
            tracked: self.tracked_count(),
            pending_new: self.pending_new_count(),
            pending_delete: self.pending_delete_count(),
            has_transaction: self.transaction.is_some(),
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Session struct implemented with all fields
- [ ] SessionConfig with sensible defaults
- [ ] add(), delete(), get() methods working
- [ ] begin(), commit(), rollback() transaction management
- [ ] flush() executes pending operations in correct order
- [ ] Identity map correctly caches objects
- [ ] Query integration returns tracked objects
- [ ] Tracing instrumentation at debug/info levels
- [ ] Debug diagnostics methods implemented
- [ ] Unit tests: 20+ test cases passing
